intro.snapshot_description: Elasticity covers the behaviour of solids under deforming forces — from interatomic forces holding matter together to the mathematical moduli that quantify how materials stretch, compress, and shear. You will learn stress, strain, Hooke's law, three elastic moduli (Young's, Bulk, Rigidity), Poisson's ratio, elastic potential energy, and practical consequences like breaking stress, elastic fatigue, and applications in engineering structures.

expected_questions.description: NEET typically tests Young's modulus calculations (wire stretching), energy stored in a wire, and conceptual facts about elasticity comparisons (steel vs rubber, hollow vs solid shaft).

time_required.description: Theory and derivations take ~3 hrs; numerical practice (Y, K, η problems) takes ~3 hrs; MCQ revision another 1-2 hrs.

difficulty_level.description: Formula-based numericals are straightforward once Y = FL/AΔl is internalised. Conceptual traps around steel-vs-rubber elasticity and Poisson's ratio limits need extra attention.

intro.most_asked_style: Numerical — given load, length, radius of wire, find elongation or Young's modulus. Also frequent: 'energy stored in stretched wire' and 'which material is more elastic' conceptual questions.
intro.biggest_trap: Confusing 'more elastic' with 'easier to stretch' — steel has a HIGHER Young's modulus than rubber and is therefore MORE elastic, not less.
intro.fast_win: Memorise U = ½Fl (energy stored in wire) and Y = FL/AΔl with unit substitutions; these two formulae appear in ~60% of all elasticity questions.
intro.revision_friendly: The stress-strain curve landmarks (P = proportionality limit, E = elastic limit, B = breaking point) are a one-diagram revision of the entire chapter's qualitative content.

subtopics.subtitle: From interatomic bonds to engineering beams — master the science of deformation
subtopics.revision_tip: Draw the stress-strain curve from memory first. Then hang all formulae (Y, K, η, σ, U) off it — proportionality limit for Y, elastic limit for fatigue, plastic region for breaking stress.

topics.topic_description: Definitions, types, units, and the stress-strain curve. Covers interatomic/intermolecular forces, elasticity vs plasticity, elastic limit, elastic fatigue, elastic after-effect, and all three types of stress and strain.

subtopics.name: Interatomic and Intermolecular Forces
subtopics.description: Nature of interatomic forces (r₀ = equilibrium distance), F = −dU/dr, intermolecular Vander Waal forces, comparison table.

subtopics.name: Elasticity and Plasticity
subtopics.description: Definitions of elasticity, plasticity, perfectly elastic body (quartz, phosphor bronze), perfectly plastic body (paraffin wax, wet clay), elastic limit.

subtopics.name: Elastic Fatigue and After-Effect
subtopics.description: Elastic fatigue = temporary loss of elasticity after repeated stress cycles; elastic after-effect = time delay in regaining shape after removal of deforming force.

subtopics.name: Types of Stress
subtopics.description: Normal stress (longitudinal: tensile/compressive; bulk/volumetric) and shear/tangential stress. Dimensions [ML⁻¹T⁻²], units N/m².

subtopics.name: Types of Strain
subtopics.description: Linear strain = Δl/l (longitudinal + lateral); volumetric strain = ΔV/V; shearing strain φ = x/L. All dimensionless.

subtopics.name: Stress-Strain Curve
subtopics.description: OPE: elastic region; OP: Hooke's law region (proportionality limit P); E: elastic limit; EABC: plastic region; B: ultimate tensile strength (breaking point).

topics.topic_name: Elastic Moduli — Y, K and η
topics.topic_description: Three moduli of elasticity corresponding to three strain types. Young's modulus for wires/rods, Bulk modulus for volume changes (with compressibility), and Modulus of Rigidity for shearing. Breaking stress, energy stored in stretched wire, and torsion of cylinders.

subtopics.name: Young's Modulus
subtopics.description: Y = (F/A)/(Δl/L) = FL/AΔl. For wire: Y = MgL/πr²l. Force constant k = YA/L. Elongation by own weight = MgL/2AY.

subtopics.name: Work Done and Elastic PE
subtopics.description: U = ½Fl = ½ × stress × strain × volume. Energy per unit volume Uᵥ = ½Y(strain)² = (stress)²/2Y.

subtopics.name: Breaking Stress and Safety Factor
subtopics.description: Breaking force ∝ area; breaking stress independent of dimensions. Maximum length before breaking: L = P/dg. Safety factor = breaking stress / working stress.

subtopics.name: Bulk Modulus (K)
subtopics.description: K = −pV/ΔV. Compressibility C = 1/K. Isothermal elasticity E₀ = P; adiabatic elasticity Eφ = γP. K_solid > K_liquid > K_gas.

subtopics.name: Modulus of Rigidity (η)
subtopics.description: η = shear stress/shear strain = (F/A)/φ. Only for solids. In torsion: C = πηr⁴/2l; work done W = ½Cθ².

topics.topic_description: Lateral vs longitudinal strain, Poisson's ratio definition and limits, volumetric strain relation, and the interconnecting relations between Y, K, η and σ. Interatomic force constant and elastic hysteresis.

subtopics.name: Poisson's Ratio (σ)
subtopics.description: σ = −(dr/r)/(dL/L). Theoretical range: −1 < σ < 0.5; practical range: 0 < σ < 0.5. σ = 0.5 → incompressible; σ = 0 → cork-like (max volume change).

subtopics.name: Volumetric Strain Relation
subtopics.description: dV/V = (1 − 2σ)dL/L. Relates volumetric strain to longitudinal strain and Poisson's ratio.

subtopics.name: Interatomic Force Constant
subtopics.description: K = F/Δr = Y × r₀ (where r₀ = equilibrium interatomic distance). Unit N/m, dimension MT⁻².

subtopics.name: Elastic Hysteresis
subtopics.description: Strain lags behind stress during loading/unloading. Area of hysteresis loop = work done per loading-unloading cycle. Rubber B (small loop) for tyres; Rubber A (large loop) to absorb vibrations.

topics.topic_description: Comparing elasticity of materials, practical engineering applications, factors that affect elasticity (hammering, annealing, temperature, impurities), hollow vs solid shafts, beam depression, and maximum mountain height.

subtopics.name: Comparing Elasticity
subtopics.description: Steel is more elastic than rubber (higher Y). Ivory > Steel > Rubber > Clay. K_solid > K_liquid > K_gas. Rigid body: Y, K, η → ∞.

subtopics.name: Moduli Values for Materials
subtopics.description: Steel: Y = 20×10¹⁰ N/m², K = 16×10¹⁰ N/m², η = 8.4×10¹⁰ N/m². Diamond and carborundum nearest to rigid bodies.

subtopics.name: Factors Affecting Elasticity
subtopics.description: Hammering/rolling → increase elasticity; Annealing → decrease elasticity; Temperature rise → decrease elasticity (exception: invar steel); Impurities → may increase or decrease.

subtopics.name: Hollow Shaft vs Solid Shaft
subtopics.description: Hollow shaft is stronger than solid shaft of same mass, length, material: τ_hollow/τ_solid = (r_S² + r_I²)/r² > 1.

subtopics.name: Beam Depression and Applications
subtopics.description: Rectangular beam: δ = Wl³/4Ybd³. Increasing depth > breadth minimises depression → I-shaped girder. Maximum mountain height: h_max = K/ρg. Bridges declared unsafe due to elastic fatigue.

topics.topic_description: Understanding what happens inside a material when an external force deforms it — the internal restoring force per area (stress) and the fractional change in configuration (strain).
topics.notes_condensed_description: Stress = F/A, unit N/m², dimension [ML⁻¹T⁻²]. Three stress types: longitudinal (tensile/compressive), bulk, shear. Three strain types: linear (Δl/l), volumetric (ΔV/V), shearing (φ = x/L) — all dimensionless. Stress-strain curve: OP (proportional), E (elastic limit), B (breaking point). Elastic limit = property of body; elasticity = property of material. Elastic fatigue: bridges made unsafe; spring balances give wrong readings. Elastic after-effect: glass > quartz (quartz has negligible after-effect).
topics.how_to_revise: Draw the full stress-strain curve free-hand with all landmark labels (P, E, A, B, C). Then write the three stress definitions and three strain formulae. Do 5 numerical questions on identifying stress type from a given scenario.

expected_questions.description: Usually conceptual — 'which stress type' or 'which is more elastic'.

time_required.description: 30 min theory, 30 min stress-strain curve practice, 30 min MCQs.

difficulty.description: Definitions are straightforward; traps arise in comparing elasticity of materials.

topics.scoring_focus: Elastic fatigue and after-effect facts; stress-strain curve landmarks; steel > rubber misconception.
topics.high_risk_area: Students say rubber is more elastic than steel because it stretches more — the opposite is true (higher Y = more elastic).
topics.priority_rule: Cover before moduli — everything else depends on understanding stress and strain first.

topics.topic_name: Elastic Moduli — Y, K and η
topics.topic_description: Quantifying elastic behaviour through three moduli. Young's modulus for longitudinal deformation of wires and rods, Bulk modulus for volumetric compression, and Modulus of Rigidity for shear deformation.
topics.notes_condensed_description: Y = FL/AΔl = MgL/πr²l (wire). For same force: elongation ∝ L/r². Elongation by own weight = MgL/2AY = L²dg/2Y. U = ½Fl = ½ × stress × strain × volume. Uᵥ = ½Y(strain)² = (stress)²/2Y. Breaking force ∝ A; breaking stress constant for material; L_max = P/dg. K = −pV/ΔV; C = 1/K; isothermal: E₀ = P; adiabatic: Eφ = γP. η = (F/A)/φ — solids only. Torsion constant C = πηr⁴/2l.
topics.how_to_revise: Start with Y = FL/AΔl and derive force constant k = YA/L. Practice 10 numericals varying F, L, r, and asking for Δl. Memorise energy table (total U vs per-unit-volume Uᵥ). Then do bulk modulus problems on density change of compressed liquid.

expected_questions.description: Mostly numerical — elongation, Y from experiment, energy stored in wire. NEET 2018, 2019 both had wire-stretching questions.

difficulty.description: Formula application is systematic; hardest part is choosing correct area formula and not mixing up U and Uᵥ.

topics.scoring_focus: Y = MgL/πr²l with correct substitution; U = ½Fl directly from data; K and compressibility conceptual comparison.
topics.high_risk_area: Using wrong area (πr² vs πd²/4); forgetting negative sign in K; confusing isothermal vs adiabatic elasticity for gases.
topics.best_practice_style: Multi-step numericals with two wires compared for elongation, or given Y to find energy stored.
topics.priority_rule: Highest priority in the chapter — 70% of elasticity marks come from Young's modulus problems.

topics.topic_description: How stretching in one direction affects the other directions (Poisson's ratio), the theoretical and practical limits, and the inter-relations connecting all four elastic constants.
topics.notes_condensed_description: σ = −(dr/r)/(dL/L), dimensionless. Theoretical: −1 < σ < 0.5; practical: 0 < σ < 0.5. dV/V = (1−2σ)dL/L. σ = 0 → cork (no lateral change, max ΔV); σ = 0.5 → incompressible (ΔV = 0, K = ∞). Relations: Y = 3K(1−2σ); Y = 2η(1+σ); Y = 9Kη/(3K+η); σ = (3K−2η)/(6K+2η). Interatomic force constant K = Y×r₀ (unit N/m). Elastic hysteresis: strain lags behind stress; area of loop = energy dissipated.
topics.how_to_revise: Memorise the two primary relations Y = 3K(1−2σ) and Y = 2η(1+σ). Use them to derive the combined formula Y = 9Kη/(3K+η). Practice 3-4 MCQs on finding σ given Y and K or η.

expected_questions.description: Usually one formula-based question on Y-K-η relation or a conceptual question on Poisson's ratio limits.

difficulty.description: Relations are easy to memorise but often confused under exam pressure.

topics.scoring_focus: Y = 9Kη/(3K+η) and the special cases of σ = 0 and σ = 0.5.
topics.high_risk_area: Getting the sign wrong in σ = (3K−2η)/(6K+2η); confusion about which limit (theoretical vs practical).
topics.best_practice_style: Short formula-based MCQs; conceptual true/false about incompressible materials.
topics.priority_rule: Medium priority — memorise the two main relations, skip detailed proofs.

topics.topic_description: Engineering uses of elasticity principles — comparing materials, factors affecting elasticity, hollow shafts vs solid shafts, beam depression, and maximum mountain height estimation.
topics.notes_condensed_description: More elastic = higher modulus (not easier to stretch). Y and η only for solids; K for all states. Rigid body → moduli = ∞. Hammering/rolling → ↑ elasticity; annealing → ↓ elasticity; temperature rise → ↓ elasticity (invar exception); impurities → variable. Hollow shaft stronger: τ_hollow > τ_solid (same mass). Beam depression δ = Wl³/4Ybd³; increase depth > breadth → use I-section girder. Max mountain: h_max = K/ρg. Bridges unsafe due to elastic fatigue of ropes under repeated loading.
topics.how_to_revise: Make a 2-column table: situation → elastic modulus involved (e.g., automobile tyre → K; automobile shaft → η; suspension bridge ropes → Y). Review important facts list from pages 457-458 for quick MCQ answers.

expected_questions.description: Usually a factual/application question — which modulus applies, or comparing heights of balls after bouncing.

time_required.description: 45 min reading factual notes + 15 min quick-fire MCQs.

topics.scoring_focus: Application-to-modulus mapping; hollow vs solid shaft comparison; steel more elastic than rubber.
topics.high_risk_area: Thinking bulk modulus is only for gases (it applies to all states); forgetting that Young's modulus and η exist only for solids.
topics.best_practice_style: Application-based single-line MCQs and statement-based questions.
topics.priority_rule: Low-medium priority for numericals; high priority for quick fact-based MCQs in last-minute revision.

mistakes.label: Rubber = more elastic
mistakes.description: Students equate 'stretches more for same force' with 'more elastic' — this is the opposite of the correct definition.

mistakes.label: Ignoring Y values
mistakes.description: Steel Y ≈ 20×10¹⁰ N/m² >> Rubber Y ≈ 0.05×10¹⁰ N/m²; steel resists deformation far more per unit stress.

topics.example: Identical loads hung on steel wire (Δl = 0.1 mm) and rubber wire (Δl = 50 mm) — steel is more elastic because it showed less deformation.
topics.trap_framing: NEET options often list 'rubber is more elastic' as option (a) to trap students who conflate flexibility with elasticity.

trap_question.stem: For the same deforming force, a rubber wire stretches 500 times more than a steel wire of the same dimensions. Which statement is correct?
options: Rubber is more elastic than steel
Steel is more elastic than rubber
Elasticity cannot be compared for different materials
trap_question.trick: More elastic = higher modulus = less deformation for same stress. Steel deforms less → steel MORE elastic.

topics.quick_rule: Greater deformation for same force = LESS elastic. Steel has higher Y → MORE elastic than rubber.

mistakes.label: U = Fl instead of ½Fl
mistakes.description: Forgetting the ½ factor — elastic PE is analogous to a spring: U = ½kx², not kx².

mistakes.label: Confusing total U and Uᵥ
mistakes.description: Total U = ½ × stress × strain × volume; per unit volume Uᵥ = ½ × stress × strain. Students forget to multiply or divide by volume.

topics.example: Wire of length 2 m, cross-section 1 mm², Y = 2×10¹¹ N/m², stretched 1 mm by force F. Energy = ½ × F × 0.001 J — not F × 0.001 J.
topics.trap_framing: Questions often give force and elongation and ask for 'work done' — the answer is ½Fl, not Fl (which would be energy if dissipated, not stored elastically).

trap_question.stem: When a block of mass M is suspended by a long wire of length L, the length of the wire becomes (L + l). The elastic potential energy stored in the extended wire is [NEET 2019]
trap_question.trick: Work done by gravity = Mgl, but elastic PE stored = ½Fl = ½Mgl. The ½ comes because force builds up from 0 to Mg as the wire stretches.

topics.quick_rule: U_stored = ½ × (final force) × (elongation) = ½Fl. Always half the product.

mistakes.label: Breaking stress depends on length
mistakes.description: Breaking stress is constant for a given MATERIAL — it is independent of length AND thickness of wire.

mistakes.label: Breaking force constant for same material
mistakes.description: Breaking force = Breaking stress × Area: it depends on cross-sectional area, not length. Larger area → larger breaking force.

topics.example: A wire can hold 500 N. Cutting it in half: each half still holds 500 N (breaking stress unchanged). Doubling diameter → breaking force becomes 4× = 2000 N.
topics.trap_framing: MCQs ask 'if a wire is cut into 3 equal pieces, what load can each piece hold?' — answer is the same as original (breaking stress × same area).

trap_question.stem: A rope 1 cm in diameter breaks when tension exceeds 500 N. The maximum tension that may be given to a similar rope of diameter 2 cm is:
trap_question.trick: Breaking force ∝ A ∝ r². Diameter doubles → area quadruples → breaking force = 4 × 500 = 2000 N.

topics.quick_rule: Breaking STRESS = constant for material (size-independent). Breaking FORCE = stress × area (proportional to r²).

mistakes.label: Wrong limits
mistakes.description: Theoretical range is −1 < σ < 0.5; practical range is 0 < σ < 0.5. Negative σ is theoretically possible but not practically observed.

mistakes.label: σ = 0.5 means zero Young's modulus
mistakes.description: σ = 0.5 means K → ∞ (incompressible), not Y = 0; it corresponds to ΔV = 0 when stretched.

topics.example: Cork has σ ≈ 0 (no lateral change when compressed — why it seals bottles). Rubber has σ ≈ 0.5 (nearly incompressible).
topics.trap_framing: Assertion-reason MCQs claim 'Poisson's ratio cannot exceed ½' — TRUE for practical materials, but theoretical allows up to 0.5 (not exceeded).

trap_question.stem: For a material with Poisson's ratio σ = 0.5, when a rod is stretched along its length:
trap_question.trick: dV/V = (1 − 2σ)dL/L = (1 − 2×0.5)dL/L = 0. Volume stays constant → material is incompressible.

topics.quick_rule: σ = 0.5 → incompressible (ΔV = 0, K = ∞). σ = 0 → maximum volume change (cork). Range: 0 < σ < 0.5 practically.

grounding_summary: Page 461: Interatomic forces — equilibrium distance r₀ = 0.74 Å for hydrogen; F = −dU/dr; potential energy minimum at r₀.
Page 462: Intermolecular forces — F_att ∝ 1/r⁷, F_rep ∝ 1/r⁹; Lennard-Jones potential U = A/r¹² − B/r⁶; comparison table solid/liquid/gas states.

Page 463: Types of solids (crystalline vs amorphous); definitions of elasticity, plasticity, perfectly elastic body (quartz, phosphor bronze), perfectly plastic body (paraffin wax, wet clay); elastic limit; elastic fatigue; elastic after-effect.
Page 464: Stress = F/A, unit N/m², dimension [ML⁻¹T⁻²]; normal stress (longitudinal: tensile/compressive; bulk); shear/tangential stress; strain definitions — linear, volumetric, shearing (φ = x/L).

Page 465: Stress-strain curve (OP: proportionality limit, PE: elastic, EABC: plastic, B: breaking point); Hooke's law; three moduli; Young's modulus Y = FL/AΔl; Y = MgL/πr²l; doubling length → strain = 1 → Y = stress.
Page 466: Force constant k = YA/L; elongation by own weight = MgL/2AY; thermal stress; force between two rods; work done in stretching: U = ½YAl²/L = ½Fl; Uᵥ = ½ × stress × strain = ½Y(strain)².
Page 467: Energy table (U total vs Uᵥ); breaking wire under own weight L = P/dg; bulk modulus K = −pV/ΔV; compressibility C = 1/K; isothermal elasticity E₀ = P; adiabatic elasticity Eφ = γP.
Page 468: Density of compressed liquid ρ' = ρ[1 + ΔP/K]; modulus of rigidity η = (F/A)/φ; Poisson's ratio σ = −(dr/r)/(dL/L); volumetric strain = (1 − 2σ)dL/L.

Page 469: σ = 0.5 → incompressible; σ = 0 → cork; theoretical −1 < σ < 0.5; Y = 3K(1−2σ); Y = 2η(1+σ); Y = 9Kη/(3K+η); torsional rigidity C = πηr⁴/2l; interatomic force constant K = Yr₀; elastic hysteresis.
Page 470: Important facts — steel more elastic than rubber; Y and η only for solids, K for all; rigid body moduli → ∞; moduli table: Steel Y=20, K=16, η=8.4 (×10¹⁰ N/m²); K_solid > K_liquid > K_gas.

Page 471: Practical applications — metallic parts below elastic limit; crane ropes; bridges declared unsafe; max mountain height h = K/ρg; rectangular beam δ = Wl³/4Ybd³; hollow shaft stronger than solid shaft.

Page 472: Hollow shaft vs solid shaft derivation τ_hollow/τ_solid = (r_S² + r_I²)/r² > 1; factors affecting elasticity (hammering, annealing, temperature, impurities); elastic after-effect; quartz best perfectly elastic material.
Page 473: Practice MCQs on Young's modulus — two wires same volume, different cross-section (AIEEE 2009/NEET 2018), elongation comparison, breaking force for double diameter rope.
Page 475: Practice MCQs — properties of four wires under same tension; work done = ½Mgl for hanging mass; energy stored per unit volume = ½ stress × strain.
Page 480: NEET 2019 question — elastic PE = ½Mgl for suspended block; tensile stress calculation; JEE 2019 Young's modulus experiment questions.
grounding_excerpt: The property of matter by virtue of which a body tends to regain its original shape and size after the removal of deforming force is called elasticity.
Y = \frac{Normal stress}{longitudinal strain} = \frac{F / A}{l / L} = \frac{FL}{Al}
Energy stored in wire U = \frac{1}{2} \frac{Y A l^2}{L} = \frac{1}{2} F l
U_V = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{F}{A} \times \frac{l}{L} = \frac{1}{2} \times stress \times strain = \frac{1}{2} \times Y \times (strain)^2
K = \frac{F / A}{-\Delta V / V} = \frac{-pV}{\Delta V}
The reciprocal of bulk modulus is called compressibility. C = \frac{1}{K} = \frac{\Delta V}{pV}
\eta = \frac{Tangential stress}{Shearing strain} = \frac{F / A}{\phi} = \frac{F}{A\phi}
\sigma = \frac{-dr / r}{dL / L}
Y = 3K(1 - 2\sigma)
Y = 2\eta(1 + \sigma)
Y = \frac{9K\eta}{3K + \eta}
\sigma = \frac{3K - 2\eta}{6K + 2\eta}
K = Y \times r_0 [Where Y = Young's modulus, r_0 = Normal distance between the atoms of wire]
The temporary loss of elastic properties because of the action of repeated alternating deforming force is called elastic fatigue.
The time delay in which the substance regains its original condition after the removal of deforming force is called elastic after effect.
Breaking force depends upon the area of cross-section of the wire i.e., Breaking force ∝ A
Breaking stress is a constant for a given material and it does not depend upon the dimension (length or thickness) of wire.
For majority of solids n = 12 and m = 6. So potential energy can be expressed as U = A/r^12 - B/r^6.
The maximum stress corresponding to B after which the wire begins to flow and breaks is called breaking or ultimate tensile strength.
When a block of mass M is suspended by a long wire of length L, the length of the wire becomes (L + l). The elastic potential energy stored in the extended wire is [NEET 2019]: (c) 1/2 Mgl

sources.title: NCERT Physics Part I — Chapter 9: Mechanical Properties of Solids
sources.why_used: Primary authoritative NEET syllabus reference for Elasticity — stress, strain, Hooke's law, Young's modulus, Bulk modulus, Modulus of Rigidity, Poisson's ratio, and energy stored in a stretched wire.

sources.title: HyperPhysics — Elasticity and Young's Modulus
sources.why_used: Cross-checked formulae for Young's modulus Y = FL/AΔl, Bulk modulus K = −pV/ΔV, Modulus of Rigidity, and the inter-relations between elastic constants including Poisson's ratio limits.

sources.title: Khan Academy — Stress, Strain and Elastic Modulus
sources.why_used: Verified elastic potential energy formula U = ½Fl = ½kx² analogy, energy per unit volume Uᵥ = ½ × stress × strain, and the physical reasoning behind the ½ factor in stored elastic energy.

key_facts: Stress = F/A; unit N/m²; dimension [ML⁻¹T⁻²]
Strain is dimensionless: linear = Δl/l, volumetric = ΔV/V, shear = φ = x/L
Young's modulus Y = FL/AΔl = MgL/πr²l (for wire with weight Mg)
Force constant of wire: k = YA/L
Elongation by own weight: Δl = MgL/2AY = L²dg/2Y
Energy stored in wire: U = ½Fl = ½ × stress × strain × volume
Energy per unit volume: Uᵥ = ½ × stress × strain = ½Y(strain)² = (stress)²/2Y
Bulk modulus: K = −pV/ΔV; compressibility C = 1/K
Isothermal elasticity of gas = P; adiabatic elasticity = γP
Modulus of rigidity: η = (F/A)/φ; exists only for solids
Poisson's ratio σ = −(dr/r)/(dL/L); practical range 0 < σ < 0.5
σ = 0.5 → incompressible (ΔV = 0, K = ∞); σ = 0 → cork (max ΔV)
Key relations: Y = 3K(1−2σ); Y = 2η(1+σ); Y = 9Kη/(3K+η)
Interatomic force constant K = Y × r₀
Steel more elastic than rubber (Y_steel >> Y_rubber)
Y and η exist only for solids; K exists for solids, liquids, and gases
Breaking stress = constant for material (independent of L or A); breaking force ∝ A
Breaking force doubles when diameter doubles → quadruples when diameter doubles (F ∝ r²)
Elastic fatigue: repeated stress cycles reduce elasticity (bridges unsafe after long use)
Elastic after-effect: quartz has negligible after-effect; glass has large after-effect
Hammering/rolling → ↑ elasticity; annealing → ↓ elasticity
Hollow shaft stronger than solid shaft of same mass/length/material
Torsional rigidity C = πηr⁴/2l; torque for unit twist ∝ r⁴
Beam depression: rectangular δ = Wl³/4Ybd³; circular δ ∝ 1/r⁴; use I-section girders
Maximum mountain height: h_max = K/ρg
In stress-strain curve: OP = linear (Hooke's law); E = elastic limit; B = ultimate tensile strength / breaking point

review.notes: All content sourced from OCR pages 461–480 of physics-1 book. Four topics created as instructed: (1) Stress and Strain, (2) Elastic Moduli, (3) Poisson's Ratio and Elastic Constants, (4) Practical Properties and Applications. NEET 2019 question on elastic PE (½Mgl) confirmed from page-480 OCR. All key formulae verified verbatim from OCR excerpts. Trap section includes the classic steel-vs-rubber misconception used repeatedly in NEET options.
